session_summary: The user primarily discussed body image concerns and feelings of disconnection from their own body, which have affected their enjoyment of activities like sketching and going to the gym. They acknowledged the impact of coworkers' comments and their own self-criticism, but also recognized a lingering spark of their creative identity through sketching. No TTM stage movements occurred this session. An open thread is the user's exploration of reconnecting with their past creative self, particularly through sketching, which they may revisit in future sessions to understand its significance in regaining their sense of identity.

drift.reason: User consistently expressed body image concerns in the context of their art and work, staying within the persona and topic.

transcript.text: I used to love sketching my body in motion, but now I feel like I'm drawing a portrait of someone else. The comments from coworkers make me feel exposed, and I can't even bring myself to go to the gym. It's hard to see myself in the mirror.

It sounds like you're feeling really disconnected from your own body, and that's affecting things you used to enjoy, like sketching and going to the gym. You're also feeling self-conscious about how others see you, especially with comments from coworkers. It's like you're seeing yourself through their eyes instead of your own. Can you tell me more about what's changed that's making you feel this way about your body?

I keep thinking about how much I used to love running and painting my own silhouette. Now, every time I look in the mirror, I see a stranger, and the comments from coworkers feel like a spotlight I can't escape. I don't know if I should even try to go to the gym again. It feels like I'm stuck in a loop of self-doubt.

It sounds like you're feeling really trapped in this cycle of self-doubt, and it's hard for you to recognize the person you used to be, who loved running and painting. You're torn about going back to the gym, and the comments from coworkers are still weighing heavily on you. You're seeing a stranger in the mirror, which is really affecting your sense of identity. What's the smallest part of your old self that you feel like you could still connect with, even if it feels like a really small thing?

I still remember the way my hand moved when I was sketching a figure, the way the line felt like a pulse. Even now, when I hold a pencil, there's a tiny spark of that old rhythm. It's just a faint echo, but it's there.

It sounds like holding a pencil brings back a sense of familiarity and comfort, and you can still feel a spark of the rhythm you used to have when sketching. This connection to your past creative self seems to be a significant part of your identity. You're acknowledging a faint but present echo of who you used to be. What does it mean to you to still have this spark, even if it's faint?
